name: Cross-Sectional Momentum (12-1)
rationale: Investor underreaction to information and delayed overreaction cause past 3-12 month relative winners to keep outperforming losers (Jegadeesh-Titman 1993).
construction: Rank stocks on cumulative return months t-12 to t-2 (skip the most recent month to avoid reversal contamination). Long top decile, short bottom decile, monthly rebalance with overlapping 1-6 month holds.
failureModes: Momentum crashes: option-like payoff of past losers produces severe losses after market declines, in high volatility, and during sharp rebounds (2009)
Post-publication decay and crowding among quant funds
Negative skew and high kurtosis understate tail risk in Sharpe terms
Skip-month omission contaminates the signal with short-term reversal

name: Short-Term Reversal
rationale: Compensation for providing liquidity against uninformed order-flow pressure, plus weekly/monthly overreaction; expected returns spike with VIX because it is conditional liquidity provision, not a constant premium (Jegadeesh 1990, Nagel 2012).
construction: Rank on prior 1-week to 1-month return; long losers, short winners; hold 1 week to 1 month; weekly or monthly rebalance; ideally industry- or factor-residualized returns in a liquid large-cap universe.
failureModes: Transaction costs and bid-ask bounce: much of the paper profit IS the spread
Inventory risk in stressed markets (August 2007 quant unwind)
Signal decays within days; any execution latency destroys it
Shorting recent winners is crowded and squeeze-prone

name: Post-Earnings Announcement Drift
rationale: Investors underreact to earnings surprises because they fail to recognize the autocorrelation of seasonally-differenced earnings, producing drift for roughly 60 trading days (Bernard & Thomas 1989).
construction: SUE = (EPS_q - EPS_{q-4}) / sigma of that difference over prior 8 quarters, or 3-day announcement abnormal return. Decile sort at announcement; long top, short bottom; hold ~60 trading days or until next announcement.
failureModes: Substantial decay in large caps post-2000s as earnings-quant desks industrialized the trade
Effect concentrated in illiquid small caps where costs bite hardest
Earnings-season clustering creates lumpy, concentrated exposure
SUE definition sensitivity (time-series vs analyst-expectation) invites data mining

name: News Sentiment Momentum
rationale: Quantified news language, especially negative-word fraction, contains fundamental information that prices absorb with a lag (Tetlock 2007; Tetlock et al. 2008).
construction: Score firm-specific fresh news with NLP sentiment; aggregate daily or weekly per stock; long high-sentiment, short low-sentiment names with recent news; hold 1-5 days (fast) or 1-4 weeks (slow); daily rebalance; novelty filter against syndicated duplicates.
failureModes: Daily-horizon profits largely consumed by transaction costs (Tetlock's own caveat)
Alpha decay since NLP sentiment became a commoditized vendor product post-2010
Look-ahead and timestamp bugs are the classic simulator sin for news signals
Confounded with PEAD and price momentum unless explicitly controlled
Stale or duplicate syndicated stories poison the score
keyPapers: Tetlock, Saar-Tsechansky & Macskassy 2008 More Than Words (JF)

name: Crowded-News / Attention Fade
rationale: Retail investors are net buyers of attention-grabbing stocks (news spikes, extreme volume, extreme returns) because of search constraints, creating temporary price pressure that subsequently reverses (Barber & Odean 2008; Da, Engelberg & Gao 2011).
construction: Detect abnormal attention via search-volume spike, abnormal trading volume, or abnormal news count; let the initial 1-2 weeks of pressure run, then short high-attention names against matched low-attention peers; hold 2 weeks to 3 months; weekly rebalance; cap borrow fees.
failureModes: Short squeezes in exactly the names shorted - meme-stock risk, GME 2021 is the canonical disaster
Hard-to-borrow fees and recalls on the short leg
Timing the inflection between attention momentum and fade is fragile
Attention proxies (search volume) are noisy and subject to revision
keyPapers: Da, Engelberg & Gao 2011 In Search of Attention (JF)

name: Low-Volatility / Betting-Against-Beta
rationale: Leverage-constrained investors and benchmark-relative managers bid up high-beta stocks, flattening the security market line, so low-beta stocks earn positive alpha (Frazzini & Pedersen 2014; Baker, Bradley & Wurgler 2011).
construction: Estimate betas (1y daily correlations, 5y volatilities, shrunk toward 1); long low-beta levered to beta 1, short high-beta delevered to beta 1, rank-weighted, monthly rebalance. Long-only variant: low-vol quintile on trailing 1-3y volatility.
failureModes: Severe benchmark-relative underperformance in strong risk-on/junk rallies
Valuation crowding and de-rating episodes (2016, 2019-2020)
Strategy premise requires cheap leverage - funding shocks hurt the levered long leg
Rank-weighting and leverage scheme flatter paper implementability (Novy-Marx & Velikov critique)
Interest-rate sensitivity of the long-only low-vol variant

name: Quality (Profitability / Investment)
rationale: Partly a priced risk premium in production-based models and partly mispricing: investors underprice profitable, conservatively-investing firms (Novy-Marx 2013; Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen QMJ 2019; Fama-French 2015).
construction: Gross profits / total assets decile sort with annual rebalance (Novy-Marx), or QMJ z-score composite of profitability, growth, safety with monthly rebalance; investment leg longs conservative asset-growth, shorts aggressive. Hold 6-12 months.
failureModes: Long droughts when junk rallies (2003, 2009, 2020-21)
Chronic sector skews (short banks/utilities, long tech/pharma) unless industry-neutralized
Definition proliferation across dozens of quality metrics is a data-mining trap
Accounting restatements silently corrupt the signal
keyPapers: Novy-Marx 2013 The Other Side of Value (JFE)

name: Calendar & Cross-Sectional Seasonality
rationale: Institutional flow timing (pension contributions, month-end rebalancing, window dressing) and recurring firm events create repeating calendar patterns (Lakonishok & Smidt 1988; Heston & Sadka 2008; Keloharju et al. 2016).
construction: Cross-sectional: each month long stocks with high historical same-calendar-month returns over the past 5-20 years, short low ones; monthly rebalance. Timing variant: turn-of-month exposure window from day -1 to +3.
failureModes: Highest multiple-testing risk of any family - calendars offer infinite sorts
Roughly 100% monthly turnover makes it cost-fragile (Novy-Marx-Velikov flag)
January and turn-of-month effects concentrated in small caps
Effects shrink after publication; institutional flow-timing regimes change

name: Pairs Trading / Statistical Arbitrage
rationale: Near-substitute securities drift apart on idiosyncratic order flow and converge as arbitrageurs enforce relative-value parity - a liquidity/convergence premium (Gatev, Goetzmann & Rouwenhorst 2006; Avellaneda & Lee 2010).
construction: GGR distance method: 12-month formation matching pairs by minimum sum of squared normalized-price deviations; trade 6 months, open at 2-sigma spread, close on convergence. Modern: cointegration tests or PCA/sector-ETF residual modeled as mean-reverting OU process traded on s-score thresholds.
failureModes: Divergence risk: pairs that should converge but never do (M&A, fraud, fundamental breaks)
Crowded-book simultaneous deleveraging - the August 2007 quant unwind
Documented secular profitability decay as the trade industrialized (Do & Faff: 0.86%/mo 1962-88 to 0.24%/mo 2003-09)
Two-legged trade doubles transaction costs per round trip
Correlation-regime shifts invalidate formation-period relationships

name: Lead-Lag Spillover (Customer-Supplier)
rationale: Attention-constrained investors fail to promptly price news about economically linked firms, so returns of customers and large industry peers predict a stock's return (Cohen & Frazzini 2008; Hou 2007).
construction: Map suppliers to principal customers via disclosure data; signal = prior-month return of the customer portfolio; long suppliers with high customer returns, short low; monthly rebalance, 1-month hold. Intra-industry variant: big-firm returns lead small same-industry firms, mostly on negative news.
failureModes: Substantial post-publication decay documented in follow-up studies
Tradeable leg is small illiquid suppliers where costs are high
Link data stale or incomplete: only principal customers above 10% of revenue are disclosed
Contract losses and link breaks are not in the data until the next filing

name: Volatility-Managed Overlay
rationale: Factor expected returns do not rise one-for-one with volatility, so scaling exposure by inverse recent variance raises Sharpe; volatility is predictable while the risk-return tradeoff is not (Moreira & Muir 2017; Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015 for momentum specifically).
construction: Overlay, not a selection signal: weight_t = c * targetVar / realizedVar_t using prior 21-126 days of daily factor returns; cap leverage; weekly-monthly rescaling. Barroso-Santa-Clara: scaling momentum by inverse 6-month realized vol lifted SR 0.53 to 0.97 and tamed crashes.
failureModes: Whipsaw: deleveraging after a vol spike misses the rebound
Requires substantial leverage (2-5x) in calm periods that may not be available
Real-time out-of-sample gains weak for many factors once leverage limits and costs bind (Cederburg et al.)
Extra turnover from the scaler itself erodes the improvement
Only materially helps crash-prone factors like momentum; marginal elsewhere
keyPapers: Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015 Momentum Has Its Moments (JFE)

name: Trend-Following Overlay (Time-Series Momentum)
rationale: Anchoring, herding, and slow capital flows make an asset's own past 12-month excess return positively predict its next 1-12 month return (Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen 2012; Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen century-of-evidence).
construction: Per asset: sign of trailing 12-month excess return (or 1/3/12-month blend or MA crossover); position = sign * targetVol / assetVol; monthly rebalance. In a single-name simulator use as a per-stock or index-level filter gating other signals - the diversified-portfolio SR of 1+ does not apply to single names.
failureModes: Whipsaw in range-bound choppy regimes (2011-2013 style)
Late entry and exit at turning points
On single stocks, idiosyncratic earnings gaps dominate the trend signal
Crowding in the managed-futures complex compresses the premium
keyPapers: Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen 2012 Time Series Momentum (JFE)
Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following (JPM)

name: Earnings Revisions Momentum
rationale: Analysts revise forecasts sluggishly (anchoring) and the market underreacts to revisions, so recent consensus-estimate changes predict returns (Chan, Jegadeesh & Lakonishok 1996).
construction: Change in consensus FY1/FY2 EPS over the past 1-3 months scaled by price, or revision breadth (#up - #down)/#analysts; decile sort, long top short bottom; monthly rebalance, 1-6 month hold; require minimum analyst coverage.
failureModes: Heavily crowded - a core input to every multi-factor quant model since the 1990s
Analyst herding makes the signal lag price momentum; must orthogonalize to know what is actually traded
Vendor data lags and restatements
Sector earnings cycles create concentrated bets
Coverage requirement caps the universe and the alpha

name: 52-Week-High Momentum
rationale: The 52-week high acts as an anchor: traders are reluctant to push prices above it even on good news, so nearness to the high predicts continued gains (George & Hwang 2004).
construction: Signal = current price / 52-week-high price. Long top 30%, short bottom 30%; monthly rebalance with 6-month overlapping holds. Lower turnover than 12-1 momentum because the anchor moves slowly.
failureModes: Shares momentum's crash profile: the short leg of beaten-down names rips in rebounds
January seasonality reverses the signal as losers bounce
Correlated ~0.7+ with 12-1 momentum, so adds limited diversification to a pool that already holds momentum
Breakout chasing is crowded retail and CTA behavior

name: Idiosyncratic Volatility (Lottery Short)
rationale: High-idiosyncratic-volatility stocks earn abysmally low returns: lottery preferences and retail overpricing combined with short-sale constraints that prevent correction (Ang, Hodrick, Xing & Zhang 2006).
construction: IVOL = std of daily Fama-French-3 residuals over the prior 1 month (canonical) or 3-12 months (more stable); quintile sort; long low-IVOL, short high-IVOL; monthly rebalance, 1-month hold; borrow-fee filter on the short leg.
failureModes: Anomaly lives almost entirely in the short leg of small, illiquid, hard-to-borrow lottery stocks - borrow fees and squeezes can take net to zero
1-month IVOL estimates are noisy and flip month to month
Heavy overlap with MAX/lottery, low-vol, and size effects - double-counting without orthogonalization
Attenuated post-publication; sensitive to weighting scheme (equal-weight flatters it)
keyPapers: Detzel et al. Then and Now (SSRN 3455609)
